title: What's LemonSignal​
content: LemonSignal is a pure Luau signal implementation with huge performance improvements and new features like variadics and the ability to reconnect a preaviously disconnected connection using :Reconnect. More on those features on the next page.

title: API Design​
content: LemonSignal uses the conventional Roblox signal and connection so you can easily swap it with any other Luau signal that uses that API like GoodSignal and immediately enjoy the new features and improvements.

title: Wally (Recommended)​
content: You will find the latest version of LemonSignal athttps://wally.run/package/data-oriented-house/lemonsignal

title: Connection<T...>​
content: </> interface Connection<T...> { Connected: boolean Disconnect: (Connection<T...>) → () Reconnect: (Connection<T...>) → () }

title: Connected​
content: This item is read only and cannot be modified. Read Only </> Connection.Connected: boolean Indicates whether the connection is connected or not. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Hello world!") print(connection.Connected) -- true connection:Disconnect() print(connection.Connected) -- false

title: Disconnect​
content: </> Connection.Disconnect(self: Connection<T...>) → () Disconnects the connection from the signal. May be reconnected later using :Reconnect(). local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") -- Test: Hello world! connection:Disconnect() signal:Fire("Goodbye world!")

title: Reconnect​
content: </> Connection.Reconnect(self: Connection<T...>) → () Reconnects the connection to the signal again. If it's a :Once connection it will be disconnected after the next signal fire. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") -- Test: Hello world! connection:Disconnect() signal:Fire("Goodbye world!") connection:Reconnect() signal:Fire("Hello again!") -- Test: Hello again!

title: Performance (Roblox)
content: Performance (Roblox) LemonSignal's :Fire uses coroutine.resume instead of task.spawn because resume is a whopping ~80% faster than spawn while behaving functionally the same. This makes LemonSignal objectively faster than any signal that uses task.spawn like GoodSignal. You might be rightfully asking what's the catch? And I am here to tell you there's none! While coroutine.resume makes debugging less convenient because the Roblox output window doesnt account for it well enough, we can simply go around it by using task.spawn only when the connection errors so the better error reporting of the task scheduler can take the wheel, best of both worlds! To measure the huge performance improvement coroutine.resume achieves, I used boatbomber's bechmarker plugin and ran the following benchmark: --!optimize 2 local N = 1000 local co = coroutine.create(function() while true do local fn = coroutine.yield() fn() end end) coroutine.resume(co) return { ParameterGenerator = function() return end, Functions = { ["task.spawn"] = function(Profiler) for i = 1, N do task.spawn(co, function() return i end) end end, ["coroutine.resume"] = function(Profiler) for i = 1, N do coroutine.resume(co, function() return i end) end end, }, } Bear in mind that the execution times I am about to show dont measure how much time a single coroutine.resume or task.spawn call will take, we are benchmarking batches of those calls to get consistent results, so we can only use these numbers relatively to eachother. Function	50th %	Averagetask.spawn	455 μs	911 μs coroutine.resume	73 μs	277 μs Using the 50th percentile we can see that coroutine.resume is ~84% faster than task.spawn!

title: Reconnect​
content: There are occasions where you :Disconnect and end up calling :Connect again with a new function, with :Reconnect that stops being a problem because reconnecting uses that exact function you passed in your connection and simply links it back to the signal. Note: :Once connections retain their behavior, as in they will get disconnected again on the next :Fire local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(function() print("Used Connect") end) local connection2 = signal:Once(function() print("Used Once") end) signal:Fire() -- Used Once -- Used Connect connection1:Disconnect() signal:Fire() -- both connections are disconnected, nothing will fire connection1:Reconnect() connection2:Reconnect() signal:Fire() -- Used Once -- Used Connect print(connection2.Connected) -- prints false, the :Once connection retains its behavior signal:Fire() -- Used Connect

title: Variadics​
content: Another cool feature is the ability to connect a function while passing varargs which, for example, allows you to use the same function for all your objects for a clean and flyweight code. Note that the :Fire varargs get appended to the :Connect varargs, meaning that :Connect's varargs will always come before :Fire's local signal = LemonSignal.new() local function foo(str1: string, str2: string) print(str1 .. " " .. str2) end signal:Connect(foo, "Hello") signal:Fire("world!") -- Hello world!

title: Signal​
content: </> interface Signal { Connect: ( Signal, fn: (...any) → (), T... ) → Connection<T...> Once: ( Signal, fn: (...any) → (), T... ) → Connection<T...> Fire: ( Signal, any ) → () DisconnectAll: (Signal) → () }

title: new​
content: </> Signal.new() → Signal Returns a new signal instance which can be used to connect functions. local LemonSignal = require(path.to.LemonSignal) local signal = LemonSignal.new()

title: Connect​
content: </> Signal.Connect( self: Signal, fn: (...any) → (), ...: T... ) → () Connects a function to the signal and returns the connection. Passed variadic arguments will always be prepended to fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(function(str: string) print(str) end) local connection2 = signal:Connect(print, "Hello") signal:Fire("world!") -- Hello world! -- world!

title: Once​
content: </> Signal.Once( self: Signal, fn: (...any) → (), ...: T... ) → () Connects a function to a signal and disconnects if after the first fire. Can be reconnected later. local signal = Signal.new() local connection = signal:Once(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") -- Test: Hello world! print(connection.Connected) -- false

title: Fire​
content: </> Signal.Fire( self: Signal, ...: any ) → () Fires the signal and calls all connected functions with the connection's bound arguments and the fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") -- Test: Hello world!

title: Wait​
content: </> Signal.Wait(self: Signal) → ...any Waits until the signal is fired and returns the fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() task.delay(1, function() signal:Fire("Hello", "world!") end) local str1, str2 = signal:Wait() print(str1) -- Hello print(str2) -- world!

title: DisconnectAll​
content: </> Signal.DisconnectAll(self: Signal) → () Disconnects all connections currently connected to the signal. They may be reconnected later. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(print, "First:") local connection2 = signal:Connect(print, "Second:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") -- Second: Hello world! -- First: Hello world! signal:DisconnectAll() signal:Fire("Goodbye World!")
